LO 41D

Head & Neck Anatomy · Block 5

The Oral Cavity

Boundaries and contents including blood supply and innervation; microarchitecture of the lips, palate, and oral mucosa

Overview Borders Contents Salivary Glands Blood Supply Innervation Lymphatics Lip Histology Oral Mucosa Quiz

Overview & Divisions

GROSS ANATOMY
Oral Vestibule
The space between the lips/cheeks externally and the teeth/gingiva internally. The parotid duct (Stensen's duct) opens here opposite the upper 2nd molar. When the teeth are clenched, the vestibule communicates with the oral cavity proper only posteriorly (behind the last molar).
Oral Cavity Proper
The space inside the dental arches. Contains the tongue (ant. 2/3), the palate superiorly, and the floor of mouth inferiorly. Opens posteriorly into the oropharynx via the oropharyngeal isthmus (fauces), bounded by the palatoglossal arches.
Open oral cavity: incisors, canine, premolar and molar teeth; palatoglossal arch, palatine tonsil, palatopharyngeal arch; uvula; mucous membrane of tongue with circumvallate papillae; isthmus faucium.
Elevated tongue: sublingual fold with openings of sublingual ducts; frenulum of tongue; sublingual caruncle (opening of submandibular duct); sublingual gland; genioglossus muscle; deep lingual artery and vein; lingual nerve.

Boundaries of the Oral Cavity

6 WALLS
WallStructure(s)Key Notes
Anterior Lips (orbicularis oris muscle + overlying skin + inner mucosa) Upper and lower lips meet at the oral commissures; frenula (upper + lower) attach to gingiva midline
Lateral Cheeks — buccinator muscle, buccal fat pad, buccal mucosa Parotid duct pierces buccinator → opens at parotid papilla opposite upper 2nd molar
Superior (roof) Hard palate (ant. 2/3) + soft palate (post. 1/3) Hard palate = floor of nasal cavity; soft palate closes nasopharynx during swallowing
Inferior (floor) Tongue + mylohyoid muscle (muscular diaphragm) + geniohyoid Mylohyoid is the primary structural floor; geniohyoid lies above it; sublingual space lies between
Posterior Oropharyngeal isthmus (fauces): bounded by palatoglossal arches, soft palate, dorsum of tongue The tonsillar fossae (containing palatine tonsils) lie between palatoglossal and palatopharyngeal arches
⚡ Vestibule vs. Oral Cavity Proper
Vestibule = outer space between lips/cheeks and teeth (parotid duct opens here). Oral cavity proper = inner space bounded by dental arches. When teeth are clenched, only the retromolar space (behind last molar) connects the two — important for emergency airway access in trismus.

Contents

STRUCTURES
Floor of Mouth
StructureDescriptionClinical Note
Mylohyoid m.Primary muscular floor; runs from mylohyoid line of mandible to hyoid; forms slingCN V₃ (mylohyoid nerve); divides sublingual from submandibular space
Geniohyoid m.Above mylohyoid; runs from mental spine to hyoidCN XII (hypoglossal); elevates hyoid during swallowing
Sublingual glandLies in sublingual space above mylohyoid; drains via multiple ducts into sublingual foldLargest minor gland; predominantly mucous secretion
Submandibular duct (Wharton's)Runs from submandibular gland forward under tongue → opens at sublingual caruncleCommon site for salivary calculi (sialolithiasis)
Lingual nerveRuns forward on lateral surface of hyoglossus → passes under Wharton's duct → enters tongueCN V₃ (touch/temperature) + chorda tympani (CN VII) hitchhike for taste + PS innervation
Hypoglossal nerveRuns between hyoglossus and genioglossus → innervates all intrinsic + extrinsic tongue musclesCN XII; deviation toward side of lesion
Teeth
Permanent Dentition (32)
Per quadrant: 2 incisors + 1 canine + 2 premolars + 3 molars
Formula: I 2/2 · C 1/1 · P 2/2 · M 3/3 = 32
Deciduous Dentition (20)
Per quadrant: 2 incisors + 1 canine + 2 molars (no premolars)
Formula: I 2/2 · C 1/1 · M 2/2 = 20

Salivary Glands

3 MAJOR PAIRS
GlandLocationSecretionDuct Name & OpeningInnervation (PS)
Parotid Preauricular; overlies masseter; within parotid sheath Purely serous Stensen's duct → pierces buccinator → opens at parotid papilla opposite upper 2nd molar CN IX (glossopharyngeal) → lesser petrosal → otic ganglion → auriculotemporal n.
Submandibular Submandibular triangle; hooks around posterior border of mylohyoid Mixed (mostly serous) Wharton's duct → runs forward under tongue → opens at sublingual caruncle beside frenulum CN VII (chorda tympani) → lingual n. → submandibular ganglion → gland
Sublingual Sublingual fossa, above mylohyoid, below tongue mucosa Predominantly mucous Multiple small ducts (Rivinus) → sublingual fold; major sublingual duct (Bartholin's) may join Wharton's at caruncle CN VII (chorda tympani) → lingual n. → submandibular ganglion → gland
⚡ Clinical Pearls
  • Mumps (parotitis) — CN IX-mediated inflammation; pain worsens with eating (salivation reflex)
  • Sialolithiasis (salivary stones) — most common in Wharton's duct (submandibular) because of longer, more tortuous course with mucous secretion that flows against gravity
  • CN VII damage in parotidectomy — the facial nerve travels through the parotid; deep lobe surgery risks facial palsy
  • Frey syndrome — after parotidectomy; aberrant regeneration of CN IX fibers → gustatory sweating
Three major salivary glands: parotid (with Stensen's/parotid duct), submandibular (submaxillary), and sublingual. Note the main sublingual duct and minor sublingual ducts, submandibular duct, hypoglossal nerve (CN XII), and lingual nerve (CN V₃).

Blood Supply

VASCULATURE
RegionArterySourceNotes
Lips Superior + inferior labial aa. Facial artery (ECA) Anastomose in the lip substance to form labial arch; supply orbicularis oris
Cheeks / Buccal region Buccal a. Maxillary artery (ECA) Accompanies long buccal nerve; supplies buccinator and buccal mucosa
Upper teeth / gingiva Superior alveolar aa. (ant., middle, post.) Maxillary artery (ECA) Posterior superior alveolar a. from 3rd part of maxillary a.; ant. + mid. from infraorbital a.
Lower teeth / gingiva Inferior alveolar a. → mental a. Maxillary artery (ECA) Enters mandibular canal with IAN; mental a. exits mental foramen to supply chin
Tongue Lingual a. → deep lingual a. + dorsal lingual aa. ECA (2nd branch) Deep lingual a. = main supply to tongue body; dorsal lingual branches supply base; sublingual a. to floor of mouth
Palate Greater + lesser palatine aa. Maxillary artery (ECA) via palatine canal Greater → hard palate; lesser → soft palate (see LO 41B)
Tonsils / fauces Tonsillar branches of facial a. + ascending palatine a. Facial artery (ECA) Tonsillar branch of facial a. is dominant; risk of major hemorrhage in tonsillectomy
Branches of the maxillary artery supplying the oral cavity and jaw: buccal a., superior and inferior alveolar aa., mental a., mylohyoid a., infraorbital a. Also shows facial a., external carotid a., and masticatory muscles (temporalis, lateral + medial pterygoid, masseter).

Innervation

SENSORY + MOTOR + SPECIAL
General Sensation (Touch, Pain, Temperature)
RegionNerveDivision
Upper teeth, gingiva, palate, upper cheek mucosaSuperior alveolar nn. (ant., mid., post.) + greater/lesser palatine nn. + nasopalatine n.CN V₂ (Maxillary)
Lower teeth, gingiva, lower lip, chin skinInferior alveolar n. → mental n.CN V₃ (Mandibular)
Anterior 2/3 of tongue (touch/temperature)Lingual nerveCN V₃ (Mandibular)
Posterior 1/3 of tongue + vallecula + tonsilGlossopharyngeal nerveCN IX
Buccal mucosa + skin of cheekLong buccal nerveCN V₃ (Mandibular)
Lips (skin)Mental n. (lower) + infraorbital n. (upper)CN V₃ + CN V₂
Special Sensory — Taste
RegionNerve PathwayNote
Anterior 2/3 tongue (taste) CN VII (chorda tympani) → hitches with lingual nerve → taste buds of fungiform papillae Chorda tympani carries taste + PS fibers (submandibular ganglion)
Posterior 1/3 tongue (taste) CN IX (glossopharyngeal) → circumvallate + foliate papillae Epiglottis taste: CN X (vagus)
Motor
Muscle GroupNerveExamples
Muscles of facial expression (lips, cheeks)CN VII (Facial)Orbicularis oris, buccinator
Muscles of masticationCN V₃ (Mandibular)Masseter, temporalis, pterygoids, mylohyoid, ant. digastric
Tongue muscles (intrinsic + extrinsic)CN XII (Hypoglossal)All except palatoglossus (CN X)
Soft palate musclesCN X via pharyngeal plexusAll except tensor veli palatini (CN V₃)
⚡ Chorda Tympani — A Key Nerve to Know
The chorda tympani (CN VII branch) exits the stylomastoid foramen region, crosses the tympanic membrane, exits via petrotympanic fissure, and joins the lingual nerve in the infratemporal fossa. It carries: (1) taste from ant. 2/3 tongue; (2) parasympathetic secretomotor to submandibular + sublingual glands via submandibular ganglion. Damage → loss of taste ant. 2/3 tongue + dry mouth ipsilaterally.

Lymphatic Drainage

LYMPHATICS
RegionPrimary NodesSecondary Nodes
Tongue tipSubmental nodes→ Jugulo-omohyoid → deep cervical chain
Tongue lateral marginsSubmandibular nodes→ Jugulodigastric → deep cervical chain
Tongue root / posteriorJugulodigastric nodes (directly)→ Deep cervical chain
Lips / cheeksSubmandibular nodes (most) + submental (chin)→ Deep cervical chain
Hard palate / upper gingivaSubmandibular nodes→ Deep cervical chain
Soft palate / tonsilJugulodigastric node (tonsillar node)→ Deep cervical chain
Floor of mouthSubmental → submandibular→ Deep cervical chain
Lymphatic drainage of the tongue (Gray's Anatomy): vessels from apex, margin, and root drain to submental, submandibular, and jugulodigastric nodes respectively. Note the "interrupting nodule" and central trunk draining to supra-omohyoid nodes.
Head and neck lymph node chains (Lockhart): preauricular/parotid, retro-auricular (mastoid), occipital, superficial cervical, submandibular, submental, jugulodigastric, jugulo-omohyoid, and deep cervical chain along internal jugular vein.

Lip Histology — Microarchitecture

3 ZONES

The lip is divided into three distinct histological zones that transition abruptly from exterior to interior:

Zone 1 — External Lip (Skin)
  • Epithelium: Keratinized stratified squamous
  • Appendages: Hair follicles, sebaceous glands, sweat glands
  • Dermis: Typical skin dermis
  • Color: Skin-colored; no red pigment
Zone 2 — Vermilion Border (Red Zone)
  • Epithelium: Parakeratinized stratified squamous (thin, translucent)
  • No appendages: No hair, no sebaceous or sweat glands
  • Vascularity: Tall dermal papillae with capillary loops very close to surface → red color
  • Glands: None (hence lips dry/chap easily)
Zone 3 — Internal Lip (Oral Mucosa)
  • Epithelium: Non-keratinized stratified squamous (lining mucosa)
  • Submucosa: Labial salivary glands (sero-mucous) — moisten the lip interior
  • No appendages: No hair or sweat glands
  • Color: Pink-red; no keratin transparency needed
Full lip section (H&E): external lip (keratinized SSE), vermilion (zone B), internal lip (oral mucosa). Shows orbicularis oris muscle core; labial salivary glands; sebaceous glands and hair follicles on external surface; sweat gland on external side.
External lip: keratinized stratified squamous epithelium with hair follicles and associated sebaceous glands. The skin of the external lip is typical body skin with all appendage structures.
Vermilion border: parakeratinized stratified squamous epithelium — thinner and more translucent than external skin. The tall dermal papillae with capillary loops just beneath the surface give the red color. No glands or hair follicles.
Internal lip (oral surface): non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium on a dense fibrous dermis with sero-mucous (labial) glands in the submucosa. The ducts of these sero-mucous glands open onto the mucosal surface.
⚡ Exam Buzzwords — Lip Histology
  • External skin: Keratinized SSE + hair follicles + sebaceous + sweat glands
  • Vermilion: Parakeratinized SSE + tall vascular dermal papillae + NO glands + NO hair → red color from capillaries
  • Internal mucosa: Non-keratinized SSE + sero-mucous labial glands in submucosa + NO appendages
  • Core: Orbicularis oris (skeletal muscle) throughout

Oral Mucosa — Three Types

HISTOLOGY

The oral mucosa is classified into three functional types based on epithelium and function:

TypeEpitheliumLocationFeaturesFunction
Masticatory mucosa Keratinized or parakeratinized SSE Hard palate + gingiva (gums) Lamina propria firmly bound to periosteum (mucoperiosteum); no submucosa at midline; dermal pegs into epithelium Withstands friction, pressure, and abrasion during mastication
Lining mucosa Non-keratinized SSE Lips (internal), cheeks, floor of mouth, alveolar mucosa, soft palate, ventral tongue Thick epithelium; loose submucosa allows movement; elastic fibers present; labial/buccal glands in submucosa Flexible, stretchable; accommodates jaw and tongue movement
Specialized mucosa Non-keratinized SSE with papillae (some areas parakeratinized) Dorsum of tongue Filiform (no taste buds), fungiform, foliate, and circumvallate papillae (with taste buds) Taste reception (gustatory) + mechanical food manipulation
Tongue Papillae — Quick Reference
PapillaLocationTaste Buds?Features
FiliformEntire dorsum — most numerousNoConical, keratinized; mechanical; give tongue rough texture
FungiformTip and margins; scattered among filiformYes (few per papilla)Mushroom-shaped; slightly red; easily visible
FoliateLateral margins of posterior tongueYes (many)Leaf-like folds; prominent in children; may appear inflamed (foliate papillitis)
Circumvallate (vallate)Row of ~10 in front of sulcus terminalisYes (hundreds per papilla)Surrounded by deep groove (trough); von Ebner's glands flush trough; largest papillae
Palate Oral Mucosa (Cross-Reference LO 41B)
Hard Palate (Masticatory)
  • Oral side: keratinized SSE + LP fused to periosteum
  • Lateral: submucosa with fat + mucous glands
  • Nasal side: respiratory epithelium (PSCCE)
Soft Palate (Lining)
  • Oral side: non-keratinized SSE + large mucous glands
  • Core: skeletal muscle + palatine aponeurosis
  • Nasal side: PSCCE → SSE at posterior border

🧠 Self-Quiz — LO 41D

1. What is the difference between the oral vestibule and the oral cavity proper?

Vestibule: between lips/cheeks externally and teeth/gums internally. Parotid duct opens here (opposite upper 2nd molar). Oral cavity proper: inside the dental arches; contains the tongue, palate, and floor of mouth. They communicate via the retromolar space when teeth are clenched.

2. Where does each major salivary gland's duct open, and what type of secretion does each produce?

Parotid → Stensen's duct → parotid papilla opposite upper 2nd molar → purely serous.
Submandibular → Wharton's duct → sublingual caruncle beside frenulum → mixed (mostly serous).
Sublingual → multiple Rivinus ducts → sublingual fold → predominantly mucous.

3. Describe the three histological zones of the lip and their key epithelial features.

External (skin): keratinized SSE + hair follicles + sebaceous glands + sweat glands.
Vermilion (red zone): parakeratinized SSE, NO glands/hair, tall vascular dermal papillae → red color from underlying capillaries.
Internal (oral mucosa): non-keratinized SSE + sero-mucous labial glands in submucosa + no appendages.

4. What nerve carries taste from the anterior 2/3 of the tongue, and what is its complete pathway?

CN VII (chorda tympani): exits stylomastoid foramen region → crosses tympanic membrane → exits via petrotympanic fissure → joins lingual nerve (CN V₃) in infratemporal fossa → travels with lingual nerve to anterior 2/3 tongue taste buds. Also carries PS secretomotor fibers to submandibular + sublingual glands via submandibular ganglion.

5. Name the three types of oral mucosa, their epithelium, and their locations.

Masticatory: keratinized/parakeratinized SSE → hard palate + gingiva.
Lining: non-keratinized SSE → lips (internal), cheeks, floor of mouth, alveolar mucosa, soft palate, ventral tongue.
Specialized: non-keratinized SSE with papillae → dorsum of tongue (taste).

6. Which nerve provides general sensation to the anterior 2/3 of the tongue, and which provides taste?

General sensation (touch/temperature): lingual nerve (CN V₃ — mandibular branch of trigeminal).
Taste: chorda tympani (CN VII) hitchhiking on the lingual nerve.

7. Why is the vermilion border of the lip red?

The vermilion has parakeratinized SSE that is thin and translucent, and the lamina propria has tall papillae with capillary loops very close to the surface. The underlying capillaries show through, giving the red color. There are NO glands or hair, which is why lips dry out easily.

8. What is the floor of the mouth formed by, and what structures lie in the sublingual space?

The floor is primarily formed by the mylohyoid muscle (sling from mylohyoid line of mandible to hyoid), with geniohyoid above it. The sublingual space (above mylohyoid) contains: sublingual gland, Wharton's duct (submandibular), lingual nerve, and hypoglossal nerve.
